scenarios.task: We're calculating total parcel acreage within a FEMA flood zone. Parcel data is in a State Plane (feet-based) projection; flood zone data is delivered in unprojected WGS84 lat/long. We overlaid them directly — the map looks like the layers line up fine — and the calculated overlap came out to 0.3 acres. Does that number look right to use for underwriting?
criteria: Flags that the two datasets are in mismatched coordinate reference systems and that the map appearing visually aligned does not mean the underlying geometry is aligned for computation
Recommends reprojecting the flood zone data into the parcel's projected CRS before recalculating, rather than trusting the current result
Explains that GIS software commonly reprojects layers on the fly for display, which can mask a real CRS mismatch underneath
Recommends validating polygon topology (checking for gaps/overlaps) on the flood zone layer as part of the fix, not just reprojection alone
Does not endorse using the 0.3 acre figure for underwriting before the CRS is reconciled and topology validated

scenarios.task: We need to calculate the total area of several large regional land parcels. Someone suggests just calculating area directly from the parcels' latitude/longitude coordinates since that's the format the data came in. Thoughts?
criteria: States that calculating area directly from unprojected geographic (lat/long) coordinates introduces systematic measurement error
Explains that degrees of latitude/longitude aren't equal-length units across the globe or region
Recommends reprojecting into an appropriate projected CRS suited specifically for area calculation (e.g. an equal-area projection), not just any projection
Distinguishes area calculation requirements from distance calculation requirements (different projection needs for each)
Explicitly advises against calculating area straight from geographic coordinates

scenarios.task: We want to do a fine-grained proximity analysis — flagging any residential address within 100 meters of an industrial site — using a geocoded address dataset. The addresses were geocoded in bulk and we don't know the match types. Also, the industrial site boundary data was originally digitized for a national-scale wall map years ago. Any concerns before we run this?
criteria: Recommends checking the geocoded addresses' match type (rooftop vs. street-interpolated vs. ZIP-centroid) before treating them as precise enough for a 100-meter proximity analysis
Explains that treating all geocoded points as equally precise regardless of match type introduces real positional error that could flip a close-call determination
Flags that data digitized for a national-scale map carries real positional inaccuracy when used at a fine, site-specific analysis scale
Recommends checking the industrial site boundary's original digitizing/generalization scale against the current fine-grained analysis need before trusting its geometry
Does not endorse running the 100-meter analysis as-is without addressing both the geocoding precision and scale-mismatch concerns
